```{python}
import polars as pl
import requests
import io
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```



```{python}
# Learn morea about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# I guess the read_json function is a little picky and it likes having the files be a local path. We can use the io package to essentially do the same thing a tempfile does in 350

# Include and execute your code here
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4missing/master/data-raw/flights_missing/flights_missing.json"
response = requests.get(url)
df = pl.read_json(io.BytesIO(response.content))
```

## QUESTION 1

1. __Create a new 'season' column.__ Define 'season' as:
    1. Winter for January, Febuary (note the mispelling), March
    1. Spring for April, May, June
    1. Summer for  July, August, September
    1. Fall for October, November, December
    1. All other rows can be assigned a value of 'Unknown' 

_Describe your results and analysis here_

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
q1 = df.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col('month').is_in(['January', 'Febuary', 'March']))
    .then(pl.lit('Winter'))
    .when(pl.col('month').is_in(['April', 'May', 'June']))
    .then(pl.lit('Spring'))
    .when(pl.col('month').is_in(['July', 'August', 'September']))
    .then(pl.lit('Summer'))
    .when(pl.col('month').is_in(['October', 'November', 'December']))
    .then(pl.lit('Fall'))
    .otherwise(pl.lit('Unknown')).alias('season')
])

```

## QUESTION 2

1. __Divide all the columns by 1000 whose name starts with num_of.__ Be sure to first filter out rows that contain problematic values for the columns like -999 or 1500+.

_Describe your results and analysis here_

```{python}
# Remove the -999 and 1500+, and convert num_of_delays_carrier to integer.
# They will have to discover the integer thing on their own, but their CSE110 coding class should have prepared them to do that or know about that kind of thing.

q2= ( q1.filter(pl.col('num_of_delays_carrier') != "1500+",
                pl.col('num_of_delays_late_aircraft') != -999)
        .with_columns(pl.col('num_of_delays_carrier').cast(int))
)

```

There are 3 ways to do the division. It would be fun for them to learn about expression expansion.

### way 1 Brute force, list each column separately
```{python}
q3 = q2.with_columns((pl.col('num_of_flights_total',
                        'num_of_delays_carrier',
                        'num_of_delays_late_aircraft',
                        'num_of_delays_nas',
                        'num_of_delays_security',
                        'num_of_delays_weather',
                        'num_of_delays_total') / 1000) )
```

### way 2 short way with regex

Students haven't learned regex, but they might know it from a different class or will have seen a little bit in the reading.

```{python}
q3= q2.with_columns(
    (pl.col("^num_of_.*$") / 1000)
)
```

### way 3 short way with polars selectors

Students haven't learned column selectors yet, but they will have seen a them in the reading.

```{python}
import polars.selectors as cs

q3 = q2.with_columns(
    (cs.starts_with("num_of") / 1000)
)
```


## QUESTION 3

1. According to the BTS website, the “Weather” category only accounts for severe weather delays. Mild weather delays are not counted in the “Weather” category, but are actually included in both the “NAS” and “Late-Arriving Aircraft” categories. __Using the columns you modified in the bullet above, your job is to create a new column that calculates the total number of flights (in 1,000's) delayed by weather (both severe and mild).__ Show your work by printing the first 5 rows of the dataset. Use these three rules for your calculations:  

    a. 100% of delayed flights in the Weather category are due to weather  
    a. 30% of all delayed flights in the Late-Arriving category are due to weather  
    a. From April to August, 40% of delayed flights in the NAS category are due to weather. The rest of the months, the proportion rises to 65%  

_No discussion needed for this question_

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
q4 = q3.with_columns(
    (     pl.col('num_of_delays_late_aircraft') * 0.3 
        + pl.col('num_of_delays_weather')
        + pl.when(pl.col('month').is_in(['April', 'May', 'June', 'July', 'August']))
            .then(pl.col('num_of_delays_nas')*.4)
            .otherwise(pl.col('num_of_delays_nas')*.65)
    )
    .alias('new_weather')
)

```

## QUESTION 4

1. __Using the new weather variable calculated above, create a boxplot showing the distribution of flights delayed by weather for each season. Describe what you learn from this graph.__    

_Describe your results and analysis here_

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
ggplot(data = q4, mapping = aes(x = 'season', y = 'new_weather')) + geom_boxplot() + labs(title = "Weather delays in a month", y = "# of flights delayed") + theme(axis_title_x= element_blank())

```

Now that the columns are fixed in polars, I need to generate emails for them to extract names form

```{python}
import random

# set seed so I can get the results again
random.seed(42)

# sample of first names
first_names = [
    # modern common
    "Liam", "Noah", "Oliver", "Elijah", "James", "William", "Benjamin", "Lucas", "Henry", "Alexander",
    "Emma", "Olivia", "Ava", "Sophia", "Isabella", "Charlotte", "Amelia", "Mia", "Harper", "Evelyn",

    # modern extras
    "Mason", "Logan", "Levi", "Sebastian", "Jack", "Owen", "Aiden", "Samuel", "Daniel", "Jacob",
    "Emily", "Grace", "Ella", "Chloe", "Aria", "Lily", "Nora", "Audrey", "Lucy", "Hannah",

    # FANCY / NOBLE / LATIN-FLAVOR
    "Maximus", "Reginald", "Octavius", "Cassius", "Atticus",
    "Evander", "Lysander", "Valerius", "Percival", "Alistair",
    "Seraphina", "Ophelia", "Valentina", "Celestia", "Arabella",
    "Evangeline", "Octavia", "Genevieve", "Isolde", "Marcella",

    # heroic / fantasy-ish
    "Gideon", "Thaddeus", "Magnus", "Ronan", "Dorian",
    "Benedict", "Callum", "Soren", "Leander", "Theron",
    "Elowen", "Rowena", "Brielle", "Felicity", "Rosalind",

    # more contemporary variety
    "Caleb", "Ryan", "Nathan", "Christian", "Isaiah",
    "Julian", "Thomas", "Wesley", "Miles", "Asher",
    "Scarlett", "Madison", "Elena", "Maya", "Violet",
    "Stella", "Caroline", "Ruby", "Alice", "Josephine",

    # a few rare but not too weird
    "Marcellus", "Lucian", "Cyrus", "Aurelia", "Helena",
    "Tobias", "Damien", "Silas", "Phoebe", "Lavinia"
]

last_names = [
    # common U.S.
    "Smith", "Johnson", "Williams", "Brown", "Jones", "Garcia", "Miller", "Davis", "Rodriguez", "Martinez",
    "Hernandez", "Lopez", "Gonzalez", "Wilson", "Anderson", "Thomas", "Taylor", "Moore", "Jackson", "Martin",
    "Lee", "Perez", "Thompson", "White", "Harris", "Sanchez", "Clark", "Ramirez", "Lewis", "Robinson",

    # traditional European
    "Bennett", "Fletcher", "Crawford", "Hawkins", "Montgomery", "Barrett", "Lawrence", "Hudson", "Hayes", "Reynolds",
    "Richards", "Armstrong", "Douglas", "Chambers", "Preston", "Wallace", "Barlow", "Dalton", "Sawyer", "Winston",

    # noble / aristocratic / fancy
    "Kingsley", "Harrington", "Ashford", "Wellington", "Beauregard",
    "Kensington", "Blackwood", "Ravenscroft", "Stormwind", "Winterborne",
    "Holloway", "Everhart", "Redmond", "Langford", "Haverhill",

    # mythic / heroic / fantasy-ish
    "Stormrider", "Ironwood", "Shadowbane", "Brightshield", "Wolfhart",
    "Starcrest", "Frosthelm", "Silverwind", "Nightwell", "Thornfield",

    # old-world / classy
    "Devereaux", "Fontaine", "Lachance", "Bellerose", "Dumont",
    "Kavanagh", "OMalley", "Fitzroy", "Ludwick", "Weiss",
    "Kovacs", "Hoffmann", "Schneider", "Ricci", "Moretti",

    # rare but elegant
    "Carrington", "Ashdown", "Marwood", "Bramwell", "Loxley",
    "Kingsford", "Aldridge", "Pennington", "Somerfield", "Hartswell"
]


# make unique 1200 pairs
pairs = set()
while len(pairs) < 1200:
    fn = random.choice(first_names)
    ln = random.choice(last_names)
    pairs.add((fn, ln))

# convert to polars DataFrame
name_df = pl.DataFrame(
    list(pairs),
    schema=["first_name", "last_name"]
)

name_df
```

Make the emails for each name


```{python}
# Set seed for reproducibility
np.random.seed(42)

# probabilities
# gmail: 60%, org: 25%, yahoo: 15%
probs = [0.6, 0.25, 0.15]
domains = ["gmail", "myorg", "yahoo"]

# create a random choice column
rand_vals = np.random.rand(name_df.height)


def assign_email(val):
    if val < probs[0]:
        domain = "gmail"
    elif val < probs[0] + probs[1]:
        domain = "myorg"
    else:
        domain = "yahoo"
    
    # Random capitalization
    case_type = np.random.choice(["upper", "lower", "random"])
    if case_type == "upper":
        domain = domain.upper()
    elif case_type == "lower":
        domain = domain.lower()
    else:  # random
        domain = "".join([c.upper() if np.random.rand() > 0.5 else c.lower() for c in domain])
    
    # Random spaces
    # if np.random.rand() < 0.2:
    #     domain = " " + domain
    if np.random.rand() < 0.2:
        domain = domain + " "
    
    return domain

# apply to Polars DataFrame
email_col = [assign_email(v) for v in rand_vals]

name_df = name_df.with_columns(
    pl.Series("email_type", email_col)
)

name_df.head(10)
```


Now I need to create their actual gmail column


```{python}
STARTNUM = 54541

# number of rows
n_rows = name_df.height

# random starting number between 0 and 90000
start_num = STARTNUM

# create sequential numbers starting from start_num, zero-padded 5 digits
seq_nums = (pl.arange(0, n_rows, eager=True) + start_num).cast(pl.Utf8).str.zfill(5)

# add the column to the dataframe
name_df = name_df.with_columns([
    seq_nums.alias("unique_num")
])

# now build the email column
name_df = name_df.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col("email_type").str.to_lowercase().str.contains("gmail"))
      .then(
          pl.concat_str([
              pl.col("first_name").str.to_lowercase(),
              pl.lit("."),
              pl.col("last_name").str.to_lowercase(),
              pl.lit("@") + pl.col("email_type")  # preserve jank
          ])
      )
    .when(pl.col("email_type").str.to_lowercase().str.contains("yahoo"))
      .then(
          pl.concat_str([
              pl.col("last_name").str.to_lowercase(),
              pl.lit("_"),
              pl.col("first_name").str.to_lowercase(),
              pl.lit("@") + pl.col("email_type")
          ])
      )
    # .otherwise(
    #     pl.concat_str([
    #         pl.col("last_name").str.slice(0,5).str.to_lowercase(),
    #         pl.col("first_name").str.slice(0,3).str.to_lowercase(),
    #         pl.col("unique_num"),  # unique per row
    #         pl.lit("@") + pl.col("email_type")
    #     ])
    # )
    .otherwise(
        pl.concat_str([
            pl.col("last_name").str.to_lowercase(),
            pl.col("unique_num"),  # unique per row
            pl.col("first_name").str.to_lowercase(),
            pl.lit("@") + pl.col("email_type")
        ])
    )
    .alias("email")
])

name_df.head(10)

```



Make an email for every respondentID 
```{python}
print(data.shape)

# trim name_df to match the number of rows in data

# add the email column to data

# check

data.head(3)
```

```{python}
# shuffle all rows
name_df_shuffle = name_df.sort(pl.col('email'), descending=True)
name_df_shuffle.head()
```




```{python}
cols = data.columns

# move 'email' right after 'RespondentID'
cols.remove("email")
new_order = ["RespondentID", "email"] + [c for c in cols if c != "RespondentID"]

# reorder
data = data.select(new_order)

data.head(3)
```

Finally I need to add ten nonsense rows above the header so they have to use skip.



```{python}
import polars as pl
import os

# Use current directory or Windows temp
temp_path = os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "starwars2.csv")
# or: temp_path = r"C:\Users\mecha\starwars.csv"

# write the normal CSV first (with header)
data.write_csv(temp_path)

# Now prepend 10 nonsense lines
with open(temp_path, "r") as f:
    original = f.read()

# create 10 nonsense lines
nonsense = "\n".join(["This,is,a,nonsense,line"] * 10) + "\n"

# overwrite the file with nonsense + original CSV
with open(temp_path, "w") as f:
    f.write(nonsense + original)

print(f"CSV written with nonsense lines at {temp_path}")

```

```{python}
data_skipped = pl.read_csv(temp_path, skip_rows=10)
data_skipped.head(3)
```

---
title: "Project 5: The war with Star Wars"
date: 2025-12-22
lastmod: 2025-12-22
weight: 5
draft: false
# search related keywords
keywords: [""]
execute:
  eval: false
---

### Background

Survey data is notoriously difficult to munge.  Even when the data is recorded cleanly the options for ‘write in questions’, ‘choose from multiple answers’, ‘pick all that are right’, and ‘multiple choice questions’ makes storing the data in a tidy format difficult.

In 2014, FiveThirtyEight surveyed over 1000 people to write the article titled, [America’s Favorite ‘Star Wars’ Movies (And Least Favorite Characters)](https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/americas-favorite-star-wars-movies-and-least-favorite-characters/). They have provided the data on [GitHub](https://github.com/fivethirtyeight/data/tree/master/star-wars-survey). We have added ficitious email addresses to the dataset.

For this project, your client would like to use the Star Wars survey data in a new marketing initiative. They would like to email survey participants with product offers, but also use the responses to build a machine learning model to predict a person's outcome based on a few responses about Star Wars movies. 

Most of the data cleaning has been done for you, but there is still some data preparation that needs to take place.

### Client Request

The Client is who performed the survey but outsourced the analitics to a 3rd party (you). They want you to clean up the data so you can: 
    a. Extract the names of respondents and their email info
    a. Validate the data provided on GitHub lines up with the article by recreating 2 of the visuals from the article
    a. Predict if a person from the survey makes at least $50k


### Data

__URL:__ [StarWars.csv](https://github.com/fivethirtyeight/data/raw/master/star-wars-survey/StarWars.csv)   
__Information:__ [Article](https://fivethirtyeight.com/features/americas-favorite-star-wars-movies-and-least-favorite-characters/)


### Readings

- [Intro to the concept of regular expression (a.k.a. regex)](http://2017.compciv.org/guide/topics/regular-expressions/regex-early-overview.html)
- Polars Cookbook, [Chapter 6, Strings](https://learning.oreilly.com/library/view/polars-cookbook/9781805121152/B21621_06.xhtml)
- [read_csv documentation](http://docs.pola.rs/api/python/stable/reference/api/polars.read_csv.html)

#### Optional Alternative Readings

- [Video on regular expression](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nxjwB8up2gI)
- [Polars Users Guide: Strings](https://docs.pola.rs/user-guide/expressions/strings/#the-string-namespace)
- Python Polars: The Definitive Guide, [Chapter 12: Introduction and Strings](https://learning.oreilly.com/library/view/python-polars-the/9781098156077/ch12.html#id136) sections


### Questions

1. __Load in the Star Wars Data.__ Sometimes data doesn't come in a clean format and has metadata listed before the column headers. Use the arguments in the read_csv function to avoid the rows that prevent a successful reading of the data.

2. __Remake the respondent names from the email column. Show the first 5 rows of the result.__ Each respondentID has a unique email they used to submit their survey. We can see that each of the emails have a different format for their names. 

Use regex to create a first name column, a last name column, and a email_domain column to your data set for each row. Show the first 5 rows of the result (you don't have to show all the columns, just the email, first_name, last_name, and email_domain).

This table should be placed either in the the Methods section or an Appendix of the report submitted for this unit (see below).

3. __Make a table that counts the number of times each domain is used.__ Note that sometimes the email domain is not lowercase. Make them uniform for clean counting.

This table should be placed either in the the Methods section or an Appendix of the report submitted for this unit (see below).

### Submission / Deliverables:

__Rather than submitting something for each task of this unit, you will submit one client report at the end of the unit that is the culmination of all the tasks.__ Use [this template](../Templates/unit5_task3_template.qmd) to create your Client Report. Answer the questions. Each answer should include a written description of your results, code cells with comments, charts and/or tables.

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as pd
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

Try reading in the starwars data. Explain why it doesn't work. HINT: Look at the csv in a spreadsheet software like Excel. 


```{python}
# data = pl.read_csv('starwars_skip_rows10.csv')
```

Now read it in correctly

```{python}
# data = pl.read_csv('starwars_skip_rows10.csv',
# skip_rows=10)
data = pl.read_csv('StarWars_with_emails.csv',
skip_rows=10)
data.head()
data.shape
```

Each respondentID has a unique email they used to submit their survey. We can see that each of the emails have a different format for their names. 

Use regex to create a first and last name column for each email.
- Make a table that 


```{python}
data = data.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col("email").str.to_lowercase().str.contains("gmail"))
      .then(pl.lit(1))
    .when(pl.col("email").str.to_lowercase().str.contains("yahoo"))
      .then(pl.lit(2))
    .when(pl.col("email").str.to_lowercase().str.contains("myorg"))
      .then(pl.lit(3))
    .otherwise(pl.lit(4))
    .alias("email_type")
])

data.select(["email", "email_type"]).head(10)
```

- Make a table that counts the number of times each domain is used

```{python}
data.group_by('email_type').agg(
    pl.col('email_type').count().alias('domail_count')
)
```

- grab the first and last names from gmail and yahoo

```{python}
data = data.with_columns([
    # everything before the first @
    pl.col("email").str.replace(r"@.*$", "", literal=False).alias("name"),

    # everything after the last @
    pl.col("email").str.replace(r"^.*@", "", literal=False).alias("domain"),
])



data.head()
```


you have to str.to_lower and str.strip >:)

```{python}

data = data.with_columns([
    pl.col('domain').str.strip_chars_end().str.to_lowercase().alias('domain')
])


# data = data.with_columns([
#     pl.when(pl.col('domain').str.to_lowercase() == 'gmail')
#     .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"\..*$", ""))
#     .when(pl.col('domain').str.to_lowercase() == 'yahoo')
#     .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"^.*_", ""))
#     .otherwise(pl.lit('aaa'))
#     .alias('first_name')
# ])

# Make first name
data = data.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col('domain') == 'gmail')
    .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"\..*$", ""))
    .when(pl.col('domain') == 'yahoo')
    .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"^.*_", ""))
    .otherwise(pl.col('name').str.extract(r"\d+(.*)$", 1))
    .alias('first_name')
])

# Make last name
data = data.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col('domain') == 'gmail')
    .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"^.*\.", ""))
    .when(pl.col('domain') == 'yahoo')
    .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"_.*$", ""))
    .otherwise(pl.col('name').str.replace(r'\d.*$', ""))
    .alias('last_name')
])


data.select('name','first_name', 'last_name', 'domain').head(5)
```

```{python}
import polars as pl
import requests
import io
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

## QUESTION 1

__Load in the Star Wars Data.__ Sometimes data doesn't come in a clean form and metadata listed before the header. Use the parameters in the read_csv function to avoid the rows that prevent a successful read.

[link to read_csv docs](http://docs.pola.rs/api/python/stable/reference/api/polars.read_csv.html)



```{python}
# Students should manually look at the data to see that there are ten rows above the data that are nonesense. If they don't use skip rows, the read_csv function fails because polars wants to infer the schema based on the first rows. Because on row 11 the data gains additional rows, the function throws the error. Simply skip 10 rows to start the schema on actual data and the error is avoided.

data = pl.read_csv('StarWars_with_emails.csv',
skip_rows=10)
data.head()
data.shape
```

## QUESTION 2

__Remake the respondent names from the email column.__ Each respondentID has a unique email they used to submit their survey. We can see that each of the emails have a different format for their names. 

Use regex to create a first and last name column for each email.
- Make a table that counts the number of times each domain is used. (take note that sometimes the email domain is not lowercase. Make them uniform for clean counting)
- Make a table that has a first and last name columns instead of an email column. Show the first 5 results.



```{python}
data = data.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col("email").str.to_lowercase().str.contains("gmail"))
      .then(pl.lit(1))
    .when(pl.col("email").str.to_lowercase().str.contains("yahoo"))
      .then(pl.lit(2))
    .when(pl.col("email").str.to_lowercase().str.contains("myorg"))
      .then(pl.lit(3))
    .otherwise(pl.lit(4))
    .alias("email_type")
])

data.select(["email", "email_type"]).head(10)
```

```{python}
data.group_by('email_type').agg(
    pl.col('email_type').count().alias('domail_count')
)
```

```{python}
data = data.with_columns([
    # everything before the first @
    pl.col("email").str.replace(r"@.*$", "", literal=False).alias("name"),

    # everything after the last @
    pl.col("email").str.replace(r"^.*@", "", literal=False).alias("domain"),
])



data.head()
```

```{python}

data = data.with_columns([
    pl.col('domain').str.strip_chars_end().str.to_lowercase().alias('domain')
])

# Make first name
data = data.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col('domain') == 'gmail')
    .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"\..*$", ""))
    .when(pl.col('domain') == 'yahoo')
    .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"^.*_", ""))
    .otherwise(pl.col('name').str.extract(r"\d+(.*)$", 1))
    .alias('first_name')
])

# Make last name
data = data.with_columns([
    pl.when(pl.col('domain') == 'gmail')
    .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"^.*\.", ""))
    .when(pl.col('domain') == 'yahoo')
    .then(pl.col('name').str.replace(r"_.*$", ""))
    .otherwise(pl.col('name').str.replace(r'\d.*$', ""))
    .alias('last_name')
])


data.select('name','first_name', 'last_name', 'domain', 'email').head(5)

```
